hypothesis: The same-day sector cap might be suppressing existing A/B alpha in strong leadership clusters; changing MAX_PER_SECTOR could release or protect alpha without adding entries, exits, LLM behavior, or universe noise.
why_not_llm_soft_ranking: LLM soft-ranking remains production-sample limited, so this tested a deterministic production-shared allocation knob instead.

market_regime_summary.mid_weak: rotation-heavy bull after accepted allocation stack
market_regime_summary.old_thin: mixed-to-weak older tape with weakest win rate

MAX_PER_SECTOR_3.note: Only survival_rate changed in mid_weak and old_thin; trade count, EV, PnL, drawdown, and win rate were unchanged.

gate4_basis: Rejected and not promoted. Tightening the same-day sector cap materially damaged all three fixed windows; loosening it to 3 was inert on executed trades and failed every Gate 4 improvement criterion.
mechanism_insight: The current same-day sector cap is not the binding alpha bottleneck. Sector clustering that survives the accepted stack is valuable enough that tightening to 1 removes winners, while loosening to 3 does not release additional executable PnL under the current slot and heat stack.

llm_metrics.blocker_relation: LLM soft-ranking remains data-limited, so this deterministic capital-allocation alpha was tested instead.

rejection_reason: MAX_PER_SECTOR=1 regressed aggregate EV by -0.6565 and aggregate PnL by -$19,578.53; MAX_PER_SECTOR=3 produced no executed-trade metric improvement.
do_not_repeat_without_new_evidence: Using candidate survival-rate improvement alone as evidence for same-day sector-cap alpha
next_retry_requires: A state-specific sector crowding discriminator that changes executed trades, not just candidate survival
Forward evidence that a third same-day sector entry has better opportunity cost than the existing selected candidate
A production-shared ranking signal that chooses among same-sector candidates rather than changing the global cap
